title: Moon Bowl
medium: Oil Paint On Canvas
description: Of all the works in this exhibition, Moon Bowl is the most cosmological. A great arching form, part skull, part celestial dome, part the curved space inside closed eyes that cradles the entire upper composition, within which Thakur has compressed a universe: collaged textures layered under oil paint, teal and red and black jostling against patches of white and yellow, figures barely legible within the visual density, the whole thing figures barely legible within the visual density, the whole thing held together by an insistent black contour that moves through chaos the way a sutra holds together a chant. And then, at the base, grounding everything: the bowl. Simple. Orange. Earthen. The contrast is not decorative, it is philosophical. All that celestial complexity rests on something ordinary and ancient, celestial complexity rests on something ordinary and ancient, something made by hand and fired in earth. Time lapses, Thakur suggests, but the vessel endures. The bowl is the body; the dome above it is everything the body has ever contained.

title: Throb
medium: Acrylic Paint On Canvas
description: The canvas is split by a hard horizontal. Above: a flat, burning scarlet field with the red of urgency, of heat just burning scarlet field with the red of urgency, of heat just before breaking point from which two figures barely emerge, drawn in thin black line as if consciousness has sketched them but not yet committed to their existence. They are present the way a thought is present before it becomes a word: probable, tentative, intimate. Below the divide: that same red deepens, darkens, tangles. Black lines pulse across it in loose, involuntary loops like the body's electric activity when the mind has stopped supervising. This lower register is not the unconscious as abstraction; it is the unconscious as sensation. Thakur has painted the gap between what we know of ourselves and what we are and he has given it a heartbeat. The title is precise: a throb is not a wound and not a pleasure. It is the body's reminder that it is alive.

title: The Long Dream of Dwelling
medium: Dry Pastel, Charcoal And Pencil On Paper
description: This small work carries the weight of epochs. A mauve dome rises at the centre, nested within larger mountain forms but this is no landscape. It is a theatre of civilisation: figures at different scales distributed across the picture plane the way memories arrange themselves inside consciousness — some large and near, some tiny and themselves inside consciousness — some large and near, some tiny and distant, some half-dissolved into architecture. A bird spreads its wings in the foreground with the urgency of a messenger between worlds. Processional columns march along the lower register like a rhythm the earth has not forgotten. The diagonal of a descending line which seems like a ramp, a slope, a path of passage cuts through the centre with geometric insistence, piercing the organic and the mythic both. To geometric insistence, piercing the organic and the mythic both. To dwell is the oldest human act: to build shelter inside time, to press one's mark into the world and call it home. Thakur renders this not as nostalgia but as the body's deep memory, the accumulated sensation of having lived through many forms, many epochs, many skins. The pastel's inherent softness, its refusal of hard edges and fixed borders, makes the whole image feel like something seen from the inside of a long meditation rather than observed from outside. We are not looking at civilisation. We are remembering it from within.
